title: 奉命谋杀
summary:
Calgary questions Marshall about potential financial motives for Mrs. Aigle's death. Marshall explains that most of her property is held in trust, leaving little for her husband, Mr. Aigle, though she had purchased a substantial annuity insurance policy for the Swedish nurse, Kirsten Lindström. Marshall suggests that Dr. McMaster, a retired local doctor and former wartime nursery health officer, may have knowledge regarding the personal lives of the residents in Sunny Corner.

In a separate meeting, Police Commissioner Colonel Finney and Chief Inspector Hueston discuss the necessity of reopening the murder investigation from two years prior. They acknowledge a mistake in the original case involving Jackie Aggar, who was convicted based on fingerprints found on a poker and money, as well as a reported threat against the victim. Although Aggar was considered a "bad character," the officials now recognize he was not the murderer. Given the high security of the Aigle residence, the investigators conclude the killer likely entered the house or was someone Mrs. Aigle trusted.

The officials struggle to identify a motive because Mrs. Aigle was a generous woman whose estate was structured to minimize inheritance tax. However, they consider new possibilities: Mr. Aigle is planning to marry his long-time secretary, Miss Gwendolyn Vane, which could suggest a motive if a relationship existed or if he desired the marriage. They also consider the Swedish housekeeper, noting she might have harbored secret resentment toward Mrs. Aigle, despite having no financial incentive to kill her due to a life insurance policy provided by the deceased.
